fuzzy_prd: hey team, we need a small DOM querying + assertion utility built out. basically the idea is that QA and devs can throw an HTML snippet at it and ask things like 'find the element the user sees as X' or 'is this thing visible' or 'does this have the right styles' without writing fragile CSS selector chains every time. it should work like that accessibility-friendly locator pattern we talked about in the last sprint — you know, find by what the user actually reads, not by div > span:nth-child(3) nonsense. also needs to handle the case where text is split across child nodes (think deeply nested spans) and still match what a human would read. for the text matching part, we want some flexibility — sometimes you care about exact casing and spacing, sometimes you just want 'does it contain this somewhere'. there's also a plain object comparison use case, like asserting a response payload has at least these fields with these values without caring about the rest. the whole thing needs to be driven from a simple JSON command over stdin so our test harness can shell out to it. refer to how we handled the normalization flags in the content-query module from the previous spike for the whitespace rules. output should be line-oriented, human-readable key=value pairs. make sure hidden inputs and ancestor-inherited hiding are both covered in the visibility check.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: locate(html: string, by: string, match: string, selector?: string): MatchResult[] | locateContent(html: string, matcher: string | RegExp, selector: string, options?: ContentMatchOptions): MatchResult[]

trigger_keywords: what strategies are supported for finding elements
which locate modes exist
what values can 'by' take in a locate operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'locate' op supports exactly six strategies via the 'by' field: 'text' (exact visible text of the element), 'label' (text of an associated <label> — returns the control, not the label), 'placeholder' (a control's placeholder attribute), 'alttext' (an image's alt attribute), 'title' (the element's title attribute), and 'displayvalue' (a control's current display value). Matching is always exact against the relevant accessible text.

trigger_keywords: how does the label locator work
what element is returned when searching by label
does locate by label return the label or the input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When using 'by': 'label', the system finds a <label> whose text matches the query string, then resolves the associated form control (via the label's 'for' attribute pointing to the control's id, or via a wrapping label containing the control). The control element — not the label element — is returned in the results.

trigger_keywords: how is element output formatted
what does a match line look like
how are elements with no id reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each located element is reported as 'match=<tag>#<id>' where <tag> is the lowercased tag name. If the element has no id attribute, the literal string '(no-id)' is used instead of an id, producing lines like 'match=span#(no-id)'. Results are preceded by a 'count=<n>' line and listed in document order.

trigger_keywords: can I filter locate results by a CSS selector
how does the optional selector work in locate
what does the selector parameter do in a locate query
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An optional 'selector' field may be included in a 'locate' request. When present, only elements that match that CSS selector are considered as candidates before the accessibility-oriented matching is applied. This lets you disambiguate when the same text, label, or placeholder appears multiple times in the fragment.

trigger_keywords: how does text normalization work for locate-content
what whitespace rules apply to the content matcher
what is the default normalization behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For 'locate-content', the default normalizer: (1) trims leading and trailing whitespace, and (2) collapses every internal run of whitespace to a single space. Both steps are on by default. A 'trim' flag (default true) controls trimming; a 'collapseWhitespace' flag (default true) controls internal collapsing. Disabling either makes the corresponding whitespace significant in the comparison.

trigger_keywords: can I do a case-insensitive or substring match in locate-content
how do I match partial text content
what does the exact flag do
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default 'locate-content' uses exact, case-sensitive matching. Setting 'exact': false switches to case-insensitive substring containment — the normalized element text only needs to contain the normalized target string. A regex target (given as an object with 'pattern' and optional 'flags' fields) is tested against the normalized text and overrides the exact/substring mode.

trigger_keywords: what makes an element hidden
how is visibility determined
which conditions count as hidden
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An element is hidden if it OR any of its ancestors satisfies any of these four conditions: (1) has an inline or computed style of 'display: none', (2) has an inline or computed style of 'visibility: hidden', (3) has the HTML 'hidden' attribute present, or (4) is a form input with type='hidden'. All four conditions are checked on the element and every ancestor up the DOM tree. If none apply, the element is visible.

trigger_keywords: is the class assertion order sensitive
what does strict mode mean for class checking
how does non-strict class checking work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'class' op is order-sensitive by default: the element must carry exactly the requested classes in exactly the requested order (the actual class list must equal the expected list as an ordered sequence). Setting 'strict': false (non-strict mode) relaxes this so the element only needs to contain all the requested classes in any order — extra classes on the element are allowed and the sequence does not matter.

trigger_keywords: how is the actual class list echoed in output
what if the element has no class attribute
what does the actual= line show for class assertion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The class assertion always emits 'actual=<class list>' as its second output line, reflecting the element's real class attribute value as a space-separated string. When the element has no class attribute at all, the line is emitted as 'actual=' (empty value).

trigger_keywords: how is whitespace handled in style value comparison
are style values trimmed before comparison
what if the inline style has spaces around the value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the 'style' op, surrounding whitespace is stripped from both the expected value and the actual parsed inline value before comparison. So 'background-color: indianred' in the style attribute will match an expected value of 'indianred' (no extra spaces needed). A property that is entirely absent from the inline style is reported with an empty value and causes the check to fail.

trigger_keywords: how does partial object matching work
what equality is used in the partial assertion
does partial check nested objects
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'partial' op checks that every key in the 'expected' object exists in 'actual' with a strictly equal value (===). It does NOT do deep/recursive equality for nested objects — only top-level property values are compared. Missing keys or differing values fail the check. Each expected key produces a '<key>=match' or '<key>=mismatch' line in declaration order, preceded by 'pass=<bool>'.

trigger_keywords: what ops are supported by the CLI adapter
which commands can be sent over stdin
what values can the op field take
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin. The 'op' field selects behavior among: 'locate' (accessibility locators), 'locate-content' (text content locator), 'visibility' (visibility assertion), 'class' (class list assertion), 'style' (inline style assertion), and 'partial' (partial object assertion). Each op maps to a distinct core domain method.

trigger_keywords: how should the visibility output look
what lines does the visibility op emit
does visibility output echo the selector
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The visibility op outputs exactly three lines: first 'selector=<the CSS selector as given>', then 'visible=<true|false>', then 'hidden=<true|false>'. The visible and hidden values are always complementary booleans. The selector is echoed verbatim from the input.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values

which op strings does the adapter support

tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'locate', 'locate-content', 'visibility', 'class', 'style', 'partial'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to how we handled the normalization flags in the content-query module from the previous spike for the whitespace rules
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'locate-content' feature's normalization options: the 'trim' boolean flag (default true) and 'collapseWhitespace' boolean flag (default true), as specified in Feature 2 of start.md, which control whether leading/trailing whitespace and internal whitespace runs are normalized before matching

context_pointers.vague_reference: that accessibility-friendly locator pattern we talked about in the last sprint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1 (Accessibility-Oriented Element Locators) in start.md — specifically the six 'by' strategies: text, label, placeholder, alttext, title, displayvalue — and the label-resolution logic that returns the associated form control rather than the label element itself

The visibility check (Feature 3) must walk the DOM ancestor chain from the target element up to the root, checking each ancestor for display:none, visibility:hidden, the hidden attribute, or type=hidden on inputs — not just the target element itself

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the existing codebase or the test cases for the relevant feature? The test files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ should clarify the expected input/output contract for that scenario.

feature1_accessible_queries.json.description: Locate elements inside an HTML fragment using accessibility-oriented queries. Each request names the query strategy: visible text content, an associated form label, a placeholder, image alternative text, a title attribute, or a control's current display value. The query returns every matching element in document order; the report lists how many were found and, for each, its tag name and id. An optional CSS selector narrows the candidate set so only elements matching that selector are considered.

feature2_text_content_query.json.description: Locate an element whose collected text content (the concatenation of all descendant text) matches a target, after a normalization step. By default the normalizer trims the ends and collapses runs of whitespace to single spaces, and matching is exact and case-sensitive. A mandatory selector restricts which elements are tested. Matching can be made substring-based instead of exact, and the two normalization steps (trimming, whitespace collapsing) can each be turned off so leading/trailing or repeated spaces become significant. The target may also be given as a regular expression tested against the normalized text. The report lists how many elements matched and each match's tag name and id.

feature3_visibility.json.description: Determine whether a referenced element is visible or hidden. An element counts as hidden when it (or any ancestor) is removed from rendering via a display:none style, a visibility:hidden style, a hidden attribute, or, for form controls, an input whose type is hidden; otherwise it is visible. The element is addressed by a CSS selector against the supplied HTML fragment. The report echoes the selector and the two complementary outcomes.

feature4_class_assertion.json.description: Check whether an element carries an expected set of CSS classes. By default the check is order-sensitive: the element must carry exactly the requested classes in the requested order. A non-strict mode relaxes this so the element only has to contain all requested classes in any order. The element is addressed by a CSS selector against the supplied HTML fragment. The report states whether the check passed and echoes the element's actual class list.

feature5_style_assertion.json.description: Check whether an element's inline styles include every expected CSS property/value pair. The check passes only when each requested property is present on the element and its value matches the expected value (whitespace is ignored in the comparison); a missing property or a differing value fails the check. The element is addressed by a CSS selector against the supplied HTML fragment. The report states whether the check passed and echoes the element's actual value for each requested property (empty when the property is not set).

feature6_partial_object.json.description: Check whether one object is a partial of another: every property present in the expected object must exist on the actual object with a strictly equal value. The check passes when the expected object is a subset of the actual object (including when they are identical) and fails when any expected property is missing or has a different value. The report states whether the check passed and, for each expected property, whether it matched the actual object.
